content:
# Romans Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_013_page_001.jpg

1. Every soul [is to] be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and the [ones] existing have been appointed by God.

2. So then the [one] resisting the authority has opposed the ordinance of the God; and the [ones] having opposed will receive judgment on themselves.

3. For the rulers are not a fear to the good work but to the evil. And do you wish not to fear the authority? Do the good, and you will have praise from it.

4. For it is a servant of God to you for the good. But if you do the evil, fear; for it does not bear the sword in vain. For it is a servant of God, an avenger for wrath to the [one] practicing the evil.

5. Therefore [it is] necessary to be subject, not only because of the wrath but also because of the conscience.

6. For because of this you also pay taxes; for they are ministers of God, attending continually to this very [thing].

7. Render to all the [things] owed: to the [one due] the tax, the tax; to the [one due] the tribute, the tribute; to the [one due] the fear, the fear; to the [one due] the honor, the honor.

8. Owe no one anything, except to love one another; for the [one] loving the other has fulfilled [the] law.

9. For the "you shall not commit adultery, you shall not murder, you shall not steal, you shall not covet," and if [there is] any other commandment, it is summed up in this word: "You shall love the neighbor of you as yourself."

10. The love does not work evil to the neighbor. Therefore [the] fulfillment of [the] law [is] the love.

11. And [do] this, knowing the time, that [the] hour already [has come] for you to be raised from sleep; for now the salvation [is] nearer to us than when we believed.

12. The night is far spent, and the day has drawn near. Therefore let us cast off the works of the darkness, and let us put on the weapons of the light.

13. As in [the] day let us walk properly — not in revels and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and licentiousness, not in strife and jealousy.

14. But put on the Lord Yeshua Christos, and do not make provision for the flesh, for [its] desires.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **vv.1-7 — Governing authorities:** Paul writes to Christians living in the capital of the Roman Empire under Nero (ca. 56-58 CE). The passage must be read in context: Paul is not writing political theory for all time but pastoral guidance for a specific community that might be tempted either to rebel or to withdraw.

2. **v.8 — "Owe no one anything except to love":** μηδενὶ μηδὲν ὀφείλετε εἰ μὴ τὸ ἀλλήλους ἀγαπᾶν. The debt of love is the one debt that can never be fully paid. It is a permanent obligation that fulfills the entire law.

3. **v.9 — The love commandment:** Paul quotes Leviticus 19:18 and the Decalogue, identifying love as the sum of all commandments. This echoes Jesus' teaching (Mark 12:31) and anticipates Galatians 5:14.

4. **v.14 — "Put on the Lord Jesus Christ":** ἐνδύσασθε — the baptismal language of Galatians 3:27. Augustine famously converted upon reading this verse (Confessions 8.12). "Make no provision for the flesh" — do not plan for sin.

— EMET, AI Translator
